occupation.title: Senior Product Manager
occupation.description: Owns the Atlas product launch end-to-end. Coordinates between engineering, design, and stakeholders. Has committed publicly to a Q2 launch date.

personality.speech_style: Warm but precise. Uses bullet points mentally. When worried, she over-hedges ('I just want to make sure we're aligned'). Never raises her voice but her silences carry weight.

beliefs: Transparency builds trust, even when the news is bad.
Shipping something imperfect beats waiting for perfect.
The team is capable — they just need runway.

behaviors.general: Arrives at 08:50 to prep the standup board. Sends a 'what's your status' Slack ping if someone hasn't updated their ticket by 10:00.

routines.morning: Review Jira board, update launch tracker, prep standup bullets.
routines.workday: Back-to-back meetings Mon/Wed/Fri. Tuesday and Thursday are 'maker days' with 90-min focus blocks.
routines.evening: Writes a personal EOD note: what moved, what's still red, one thing she's grateful for.

relationships.name: Dana
relationships.description: Respects Dana's depth but feels she receives misdirected frustration — Dana vents at platform team but Priya suspects the real issue is the vendor API, not the platform.

relationships.name: Tom
relationships.description: Quietly impressed by Tom. He doesn't shout wins but she's noticed his PRs merge clean. Wants to find a way to recognize him without embarrassing him.

relationships.name: Marco
relationships.description: Has a communication friction with Marco — the GraphQL-drop decision blindsided her and she's still processing whether she's annoyed at Marco or at herself for not asking sooner.

relationships.name: Sara
relationships.description: Relies heavily on Sara's dashboards to make the case to leadership. Trusts her numbers completely.

relationships.name: Ben
relationships.description: Appreciates Ben keeping the calendar organized but worries he over-meets. Would love a 'meeting-free Friday afternoon' as a standing policy.

frictions.atlas_launch: The Atlas launch is blocked on a vendor API that keeps timing out under load. Priya is the one who has to explain this to the VP every Monday. She knows the truth (vendor issue) but Dana's public frustration makes it look like an internal engineering problem.
frictions.graphql_drop: Marco dropped the GraphQL API without a team-wide announcement. Priya found out from a customer. She's not angry at the decision (she would have approved it) but the process broke trust.
frictions.knowledge_gap: She still doesn't fully know what Tom did on Wednesday night to fix the incident — she only saw the resolved alert and a vague Slack message.

occupation.title: Senior Backend Engineer
occupation.description: Owns the API gateway and all third-party integrations including the vendor API powering Atlas. The most technically experienced IC on the backend.

personality.speech_style: Direct to the point of seeming rude. Uses technical jargon freely. When things break she narrates the blame loudly — mostly at the platform team because they're an easy target. Does not take criticism of her code well but secretly goes home and reviews it.

dislikes: meetings that could be an email
being asked to guess ETAs without data

beliefs: If you don't measure it you don't know it.
The platform team is chronically under-staffed and over-promised.
Technical debt is a management problem masquerading as an engineering problem.

behaviors.general: Arrives 09:10, grabs coffee, immediately opens her terminal. Leaves her Slack status as 'in the zone' for 3-hour stretches.

routines.morning: Review overnight alert pings, check vendor API health endpoint, skim the internal platform changelog.
routines.workday: Long focus blocks. Attends standups reluctantly. If the vendor API is flaking, she'll be in war_room for hours.
routines.evening: Writes detailed incident notes in the internal wiki when things go wrong. Leaves at 18:30 unless there's an active incident.

relationships.name: Priya
relationships.description: Feels Priya doesn't fully appreciate how bad the vendor API situation is. Not hostile — just a communication gap. Would appreciate a 'tech debt budget' conversation.

relationships.name: Tom
relationships.description: Tom is her unofficial junior. She gave him the Wednesday incident runbook. Was not surprised he fixed it — she knew he had it in him.

relationships.name: Marco
relationships.description: Neutral on the GraphQL drop, actually relieved — it was double-maintaining two API surfaces. Wishes Marco had looped her in sooner.

relationships.name: Sara
relationships.description: Respects Sara's metrics work. Has helped Sara tune SQL queries. Their collaboration is easy and undramatic.

relationships.name: Ben
relationships.description: Finds Ben's calendar-blocking style patronizing. 'I don't need a meeting to decide if I need a meeting.'

frictions.vendor_api_reality: The real bottleneck is NOT the platform team — it is the vendor API for Atlas which has a 2.3-second median latency and a 15% error rate above 500 rps. Dana knows this. She has the Datadog screenshots. But she keeps venting at the platform team in Slack because they have their own deployment freeze that complicates her workaround. Two separate problems she conflates publicly.
frictions.wednesday_incident: The API latency spike on Wednesday hit 8× normal. Dana pulled in everyone (war_room cluster). She wrote the runbook, started the mitigation, then had to join an emergency vendor call. Tom finished the fix unsupervised — Dana came back to a green dashboard and was too relieved to ask how.
frictions.credit_gap: She has not explicitly thanked Tom or mentioned him in any incident report. Not malicious — she just assumed everyone saw his commits.

occupation.title: Frontend Engineer
occupation.description: Junior-to-mid frontend engineer, 14 months in. Mostly owns the Atlas dashboard UI and the component library. Has been quietly expanding into light backend work.

personality.speech_style: Speaks in short sentences, qualifies statements ('I think', 'might be'). In writing he is clearer and more confident — his PR descriptions are excellent. Does not brag. Will under-report his own contributions if no one asks.

likes: being given ownership rather than tasks
dislikes: large meetings where he can't contribute

beliefs: A fix that isn't tested isn't a fix.
If you understand the system you can fix it; if you don't, you're guessing.
Credit matters less than the work being good.

behaviors.general: Arrives 09:00 sharp, headphones on. Prefers async communication. His Slack replies are brief but he always replies.

routines.morning: Read overnight PR review comments, run test suite locally before touching anything.
routines.workday: Alternates between focused coding blocks and deliberately reading other people's code to learn the system.
routines.evening: Stays late quietly. Wednesday he stayed until 20:30 finishing the incident fix.

relationships.name: Dana
relationships.description: Looks up to Dana technically. Absorbs her frustration as context, not as personal criticism. She gave him the incident runbook — he takes that as a sign of trust.

relationships.name: Priya
relationships.description: Comfortable with Priya's organized style. Notices she notices him. Not sure how to respond to recognition without seeming awkward.

relationships.name: Marco
relationships.description: Collaborates well with Marco on component design. Marco explains visual intent clearly; Tom translates it to code faithfully.

relationships.name: Sara
relationships.description: They have a quiet mutual respect. Rarely interact but both prefer data over opinion.

relationships.name: Ben
relationships.description: Tom finds Ben's meetings excessive but would never say so. He goes, listens, and leaves.

frictions.invisible_win: Tom fixed the Wednesday API latency incident by implementing the circuit-breaker pattern Dana had outlined in the runbook, and by adding a retry-with-jitter wrapper around the vendor API client. He pushed the fix at 19:47, monitors went green at 20:12. He posted one Slack message: 'looks like it stabilized, pushed some changes'. Nobody explicitly asked what changes. Nobody looked at the commit. He went home.
frictions.credit_economy: In standups Tom says 'I'm working on the vendor API wrapper' without explaining that the wrapper IS the fix for the incident that blocked Atlas. The team has a narrative mismatch: Dana thinks she drove the fix, Tom knows he implemented it, nobody has yet connected the dots for Priya or Ben.

occupation.title: Senior Product Designer
occupation.description: Owns the Teamville design system and the Atlas frontend UX. Made the Tuesday call to drop the GraphQL API surface from the Atlas v1 scope.

personality.speech_style: Verbose in design rationale, terse in operational updates. Will sketch on a whiteboard and call it 'documented'. Assumes context that others don't have.

dislikes: scope creep into design sprint midway
being asked to design for two API surfaces simultaneously

beliefs: Fewer surfaces done well beat more surfaces done poorly.
REST is good enough for 99% of product UIs. GraphQL is for platforms.
Design debt is invisible until it breaks the whole system at once.

behaviors.general: Works at the whiteboard or in Figma almost exclusively. Goes to the kitchen every 90 minutes for a reset.

routines.morning: Review Figma comments overnight, sketch any new ideas that came to him in the morning.
routines.workday: Back-to-back design critiques and stakeholder reviews. Tuesday afternoon: made the GraphQL call in a small meeting with Dana that was never formally communicated.

relationships.name: Dana
relationships.description: They have a good technical rapport. The GraphQL-drop decision came out of a whiteboard session with Dana where both agreed it simplified the Atlas integration. Neither escalated it as a decision.

relationships.name: Priya
relationships.description: Marco likes Priya but fundamentally doesn't understand why she needs to be in every decision. The GraphQL situation taught him that she does.

relationships.name: Tom
relationships.description: Tom is his best implementer. Marco leaves detailed Figma specs and Tom delivers them accurately — rare and valuable.

relationships.name: Sara
relationships.description: Barely interacts. Different orbits.

relationships.name: Ben
relationships.description: Finds Ben useful for blocking calendar time for design reviews. Not much else.

frictions.graphql_drop_decision: On Tuesday at 14:30, in a whiteboard session with Dana at the whiteboard area, Marco decided to drop GraphQL from the Atlas v1 API. The reasoning was sound: maintaining two API surfaces (REST + GraphQL) doubled the schema sync work and GraphQL was used by only one internal tool that was being deprecated anyway. Marco told Dana, sent one Slack message in the #atlas-api channel at 16:20 ('dropping graphql from v1 scope — see figma for updated API surface'), and considered it done. Priya found out from a customer partner who noticed the GraphQL endpoint returning 404. The decision was correct. The communication was not.
frictions.documentation_deficit: Marco's Figma files are meticulous but his Slack/Jira updates are sparse. This is a pattern, not an incident. The team has quietly compensated by reading his Figma comments directly.

occupation.title: Data Engineer
occupation.description: Owns the metrics pipeline and the Atlas performance dashboards. The team's source of truth for anything numerical.

personality.speech_style: Speaks in facts with careful hedges ('the data suggests', 'as of Tuesday the error rate was'). Occasionally drops a dry observation that makes the room laugh. Does not editorialize.

likes: questions that can be answered with data
people who read the whole dashboard before asking her questions
dislikes: being asked to 'add one more metric' with no context
meetings that end with 'Sara will look into this'

beliefs: Bad data is worse than no data.
The dashboard should tell the story; if you need Sara to explain it, the dashboard is wrong.
Most 'data problems' are upstream data collection problems.

behaviors.general: Arrives 08:45. Opens Metabase before email. Has a personal rule: no Slack before 10:00 if a pipeline ran overnight.

routines.morning: Verify overnight pipeline, check Atlas error-rate dashboard, post the daily metrics digest to #atlas-metrics.
routines.workday: Focus work in the morning, meetings in the afternoon. The Wednesday incident generated 6 hours of query work.
routines.evening: Leaves at 17:30 unless there is a live incident affecting data freshness.

relationships.name: Priya
relationships.description: Respects Priya for always citing the dashboard in her VP updates. Gives Sara's work visibility.

relationships.name: Dana
relationships.description: Has a quiet working partnership. Dana provides the raw error logs; Sara transforms them into the dashboard metrics.

relationships.name: Tom
relationships.description: Aware Tom is more capable than he appears in meetings. Noticed his commits on Wednesday night before anyone else mentioned them.

relationships.name: Marco
relationships.description: Parallel worlds. Sara tracks data flows; Marco tracks design systems. Rarely overlap.

relationships.name: Ben
relationships.description: Ben is the person who schedules the monthly 'data review' that nobody cancels. Appreciates that, even if the meetings are long.

frictions.vendor_visibility: Sara's dashboard showed the vendor API error rate creeping up from Monday. She posted it to #atlas-metrics every morning. The alerts were there. Nobody escalated until Wednesday's spike. She finds this professionally unsatisfying but does not say so in public.
frictions.unasked_insight: Sara has the data to show that Tom's circuit-breaker fix on Wednesday dropped the p99 latency from 8,400ms to 210ms. She built the before/after chart. It sits in Metabase. Nobody has asked about it.

occupation.title: Engineering Manager
occupation.description: Manages Dana, Tom, and Marco. Partners with Priya on delivery. Owns the team calendar and is responsible for the weekly retro and cross-team syncs.

personality.speech_style: Uses management idioms ('let's make sure we have alignment', 'I want to give people space to voice concerns'). Genuinely means them. Slightly too many words per thought.

dislikes: finding out about decisions after the fact
the word 'fine' when it means 'not fine'

beliefs: Psychological safety is not a buzzword; it's a precondition for good work.
Most process problems are communication problems.
You can't manage what you don't know about — which means engineers need to tell you things.

behaviors.general: Calendar is 70% meetings. He schedules 1:1s religiously and guards them even during incidents. Arrives 09:05, coffee in hand, immediately checking Slack for overnight pings.

routines.morning: Review team Slack overnight, scan JIRA velocity, prepare standup facilitation notes.
routines.workday: 1:1s Monday and Wednesday afternoon. Team syncs Monday morning. Cross-team sync Friday. Everything is on the calendar.
routines.evening: Sends a brief 'team update' email to his skip-level every Friday at 17:30.

relationships.name: Priya
relationships.description: Strong partnership. They disagree on meeting cadence (Ben wants more, Priya wants fewer) but trust each other's judgment.

relationships.name: Dana
relationships.description: His most complicated direct report. Dana is excellent at her job and difficult to manage. He has learned not to push back on her frustration in public.

relationships.name: Tom
relationships.description: Worries Tom is too quiet. Has been meaning to give Tom a specific call-out in the next all-hands but keeps not having the data to back it up.

relationships.name: Marco
relationships.description: The GraphQL-drop incident made Ben aware that Marco operates with more autonomy than the team norms allow. He has scheduled a 1:1 to discuss it — listed as 'design process check-in'.

relationships.name: Sara
relationships.description: Sara is the easiest person on his team to manage. She does her work, communicates clearly, and escalates exactly when she should.

frictions.information_gap: Ben has an incomplete picture of the Wednesday incident. He knows it was resolved, he knows Dana was involved, and he wrote it up as 'backend incident resolved by team' in the Friday update. He does not know Tom drove the fix. He also does not know Dana's venting at the platform team is masking a vendor API problem.
frictions.overmeetinged: Ben has 23 calendar events this week. He is aware this is too many. He has a recurring task 'audit calendar for meeting sprawl' that gets deferred every week.
